complainant__contractor_information.who_presented_negotiated_or_explained_th: Jason Miller (Sales Representative for SunPeak Renewable Solutions, Inc.)

authorized_assistant__how_contractor_was.where_was_the_contract_negotiated: At my kitchen table at 1448 W 82nd Street, Los Angeles, CA 90047

complaint_details.pace_provider_used: GreenCity PACE Finance Program (through Los Angeles County)
complaint_details.remedy_sought:
I am seeking: (1) correction of all defective and incomplete work on my solar system and roof at no additional cost to me by a properly licensed and reputable contractor approved by CSLB; (2) reimbursement or cancellation of the Property Assessed Clean Energy (PACE) financing that was arranged based on what I believe were misleading statements about my payments and savings; and (3) any additional restitution or penalties that CSLB determines are appropriate based on the contractor’s conduct.

More specifically:

1. COMPLETION AND REPAIR OF WORK: The solar system on my roof is currently only partially operational and appears to have been installed incorrectly in several places. There are exposed conduit runs, loose wiring on the side of my house, and mounting brackets that are not fully sealed to the roof. Since the installation, I have had two roof leaks during light rain (on 2024-05-27 and 2024-08-03) in the hallway ceiling directly under where some of the panels were mounted. I had a local roofer (not affiliated with the contractor) look at it, and he told me that some of the flashing and penetrations were not properly sealed. I would like a qualified, independent, licensed contractor to assess the entire system and roof, repair all damage caused by the installation, and bring the solar installation up to code and to the standards promised in the contract, without me having to pay anything additional.

2. ADDRESSING ABANDONMENT: Work essentially stopped on or about 2024-05-22. Since then, the contractor has not returned to finish the job, even though I have repeatedly called and emailed. The monitoring system is not fully set up, and the inverter frequently shows error codes. The city inspection was never passed, so my system is not legally approved for interconnection, yet I am already being charged on my property tax bill for the PACE assessment. I want CSLB to require the contractor to either come back and finish the work properly and pass inspection or, if they are unwilling or unable, to arrange for another contractor to do so at their expense.

3. MISREPRESENTATION AND FINANCING ISSUES: The salesperson, Jason Miller, told me that my out-of-pocket cost would be “basically nothing” and that the PACE payments would be “covered by the savings on your electric bill.” He said my LADWP bill would drop to around $20–$30 per month and that the PACE assessment would be about $140 per month added to my property taxes. In reality, the assessment that showed up on my preliminary property tax statement is closer to $265 per month over 20 years, and my electric bill has only gone down by about $40–$60 per month because the system is not fully working. He also told me that if I decided I did not like the program within the first year, I could “just cancel it,” which I have since learned is not accurate because paying off the PACE loan early requires a large lump-sum payment that I do not have.

I feel that I was pressured into signing the documents quickly. Jason brought an electronic tablet and scrolled through multiple pages for me to sign, saying, “This is all standard language; it just confirms what we talked about.” He did not leave me copies that same day; I only received the full contract and financing documents by email a couple of days later. By the time I realized the actual payment amount and terms, the contractor had already started installing the system. I am asking CSLB to investigate whether the contractor and/or their salesperson misrepresented the terms of the contract and the financing and to help me unwind or modify the PACE assessment if it was obtained through deceptive practices.

4. FINANCIAL RELIEF: Because of this situation, I am now facing higher overall monthly costs (PACE assessment plus still relatively high electric bills) than before I installed solar, which is the opposite of what was promised. I am a retired school secretary on a fixed income (Social Security and a small pension), and I cannot afford to pay for a defective solar system that does not provide the promised savings. I would like the PACE financing either canceled entirely or reduced to reflect a fair value for a properly functioning system—after all defects are corrected. If cancellation is not possible, I would like CSLB’s assistance in negotiating with the contractor and the PACE provider to either significantly reduce the principal balance or have the contractor reimburse me for the difference between what was promised and what I am actually paying.

5. ACCOUNTABILITY AND COMMUNICATION: Since problems began, I have repeatedly tried to get help. I called the contractor’s main office at least seven times between June and September 2024 and sent multiple emails to both Jason Miller and the general support email. I often received no response, or I was told someone would call me back, which rarely happened. On one occasion, they scheduled a technician, but he canceled the appointment the morning of and never rescheduled. I would like CSLB to hold the contractor accountable for this lack of follow-through and require them to respond promptly and in writing with a clear plan to resolve these issues.

In summary, I am asking CSLB to help ensure: (a) the system and my roof are repaired and completed properly; (b) any misrepresentations regarding cost and financing are addressed, with appropriate adjustments or cancellations of the PACE assessment; and (c) the contractor is held responsible for any violations of licensing, advertising, contract, or consumer protection rules that may apply in this situation.
